title: Branching under First-Passage Resetting
abstract: Many biological processes, from cell division to viral lysis, are triggered when an internal stochastic variable reaches a threshold. Here we introduce Branching under First-Passage Resetting, a general framework in which replication events arise endogenously from first-passage dynamics rather than from externally imposed lifetime clocks. We show that the resulting population dynamics obey an exact renewal equation linking single-trajectory first-passage statistics to the population growth rate. This mapping shows that, for fixed offspring number and fixed mean replication time, stochastic timing fluctuations necessarily enhance growth relative to a deterministic clock. When offspring yield depends on the first-passage time, however, fluctuations have non-trivial effects and expose a fundamental yield-delay trade-off: waiting longer can increase the number of descendants, but delays all future lineages. Our framework allows us to address this optimization problem analytically, and upon application to bacteriophage lysis, gives an optimal lysis time and growth rate consistent with empirical data.
summary: This paper introduces a framework for branching processes where replication is triggered by an internal stochastic variable reaching a threshold. It demonstrates that timing fluctuations can optimize population growth by balancing the benefit of higher offspring yield against the cost of delayed reproduction. The theory is successfully validated against experimental data from bacteriophage lysis.
main_problem: Understanding how stochastic fluctuations in replication timing, driven by internal first-passage dynamics, affect the macroscopic population growth rate and the trade-off between offspring yield and reproduction delay.
main_result: The authors derive an exact renewal equation showing that for fixed yields, timing fluctuations necessarily enhance growth, while for time-dependent yields, fluctuations can either enhance or reduce growth depending on a specific yield-delay trade-off.
method: The study uses a renewal approach, Laplace transforms, and a generalized Euler-Lotka equation, supplemented by small-fluctuation expansions and the use of the Lambert W function for analytical optimization.
model_or_system: A branching process where replication events are triggered when an internal stochastic variable reaches a threshold (First-Passage Resetting). Models include drift-diffusion processes and multi-state Erlang/Gamma chains, with applications to bacteriophage lysis.
key_observables: Population growth rate (lambda), first-passage time (TL), offspring number/yield (m), and the coefficient of variation (CV^2) of the timing.
important_parameters: Threshold (L), drift velocity (vd), diffusion constant (D), power-law yield exponent (beta), and the yield-delay trade-off regime.
main_assumptions: Assumes the existence of a probability density for first-passage times and that the population dynamics can be described by a generalized Bellman-Harris/Euler-Lotka framework.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the core model and phage lysis application; Figure 2 compares population density and growth rate vs. variability for different stochastic clocks; Figure 3 illustrates the impact of diffusion on growth.
paper_structure: Introduction of the BFPR framework, derivation of the exact renewal equation, analysis of fixed-yield vs. time-dependent yield regimes, optimization of the yield-delay trade-off, and experimental validation using bacteriophage data.
why_interesting: The framework of first-passage resetting and the mapping between microscopic stochasticity and macroscopic growth rates are highly relevant to the study of non-equilibrium dynamics and the stability of populations in open quantum systems.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Branching under first-passage resetting. (a) Schematic showing the core model for m = 2. Trajectories stochastically evolve until the threshold is reached at which point the trajectory simultaneously resets to the origin and branches. (b) Processes such as viral lysis can be modeled under our framework, with distributions over the lysis time, fL(t), and a time-dependent number of offspring, m(t).

FIG. 2. (a) The density ρ(x, t) measured at several posi- tions for a drift-diffusion process with close agreement be- tween the simulations (symbols), exact theory (solid curve) and the asymptotic form (dashed line) in Eq. (6). (b) Growth rate λ as a function of the first-passage-time vari- ability CV2 = Var(TL)/⟨TL⟩2, at fixed mean ⟨TL⟩and fixed offspring number m = 2 for representative processes. Increas- ing CV2 increases λ. For the discrete multi-state chain, sim- ulation points correspond to Erlang clocks with an integer number n of states, for which CV2 = 1/n; hence the markers are not uniformly spaced. Model details and parameters are given in Ref. [53].

FIG. 3. Time-dependent yield and the yield-delay trade-off. (a) Change in growth rate, ∆λ = λ −λdet, as a function of the first-passage-time variability CV2, for drift–diffusion clocks with fixed mean ⟨TL⟩= 1 and fixed yield at the mean, m(⟨TL⟩) = 3. For power-law yields m(t) = m0tβ, timing fluctuations can either enhance or suppress growth: they increase λ for β = 0.2 but decrease it for β = 1.2. Symbols denote simulations, solid curves denote the exact solution of the generalized Euler–Lotka equation, and the small-fluctuation approximation (dashed line) agrees near the deterministic limit. The exponential-yield control m(t) = eωt, with ω = ln 3, gives ∆λ = 0 for all CV2 [53]. (b)...
